notes: Cluster assignments are moderately stable across reruns.
The most repeatable regime pattern is fragile leo constellation traffic, appearing in 5 of 5 runs.

takeaways: The cross-orbit regime is not unique to the autoencoder, so that claim should be treated as shared structure rather than purely neural discovery.
The autoencoder also yields the strongest average separation score in its own learned space.
PCA baseline is more seed-stable than the autoencoder, so the neural model should be framed as a tradeoff for structure quality rather than raw robustness.
The anomaly rankings still deserve caution because the simpler baselines are at least as clean on source-coverage behavior.

regimes.label: Fragile LEO constellation traffic
regimes.description: This regime is mostly LEO, skews toward communications constellation, and looks structurally fragile. Its weakest shared signal is shared traffic pressure, with an average anomaly score of 0.0017.

takeaways: Cross-orbit structure already appears in the orbital-only view, but traffic and debris features make it richer and closer to the primary result.
The regime structure survives without mission/operator context, so the main finding is not just metadata clustering.
The main fragility patterns still appear when stewardship proxy inputs are removed, which makes the orbital-condition claim more defensible.
At least part of the regime structure survives on direct and derived features alone, without the proxy-heavy inputs.
Only a small subset of the regimes survives on direct and derived features alone, so the proxy-heavy findings need careful framing.
Removing stewardship proxies does not erase the main traffic/debris structure.
Mission/operator context helps separate regimes, but it is acting as a sharpening signal rather than the sole driver.

qualityNotes: Global outliers are ranked by reconstruction error from the learned autoencoder.
Within-regime outliers compare each satellite against the peers in its learned vulnerability regime.
Within-orbit outliers compare each satellite against peers that share the same orbit label.

regimeInterpretations.interpretation: This regime is defined by higher congestion penalty (0.6σ), higher owner population (0.6σ), and higher owner share (0.6σ). That combination pulls hardest on congestion impact and leaves the group at 63.69/100 on average. It appears consistently across reruns. The pattern survives when the stewardship proxies are removed, but not when all proxy features are stripped away.

This regime is defined by lower orbit population (1.6σ), lower orbit share (1.6σ), and higher debris shell penalty (1.5σ). That combination pulls hardest on debris exposure and leaves the group at 66.52/100 on average. It appears consistently across reruns. The pattern survives when the stewardship proxies are removed, but not when all proxy features are stripped away.

This regime is defined by higher mission complexity (1.6σ), lower owner population (1.2σ), and lower owner share (1.2σ). That combination pulls hardest on congestion impact and leaves the group at 61.29/100 on average. It appears consistently across reruns. The pattern still appears when the proxy-heavy features are removed.

This regime is defined by lower mean motion (3.9σ), higher perigee (3.8σ), and higher mean altitude (3.4σ). That combination pulls hardest on sustainability and leaves the group at 79.63/100 on average. It shows up often, but not in every rerun. The pattern weakens once the proxy-heavy inputs are removed, so it should be read cautiously.

This regime is defined by higher eccentricity (14.9σ), higher altitude span (11.4σ), and lower shell density (8.4σ). That combination pulls hardest on debris exposure and leaves the group at 61.19/100 on average. It appears consistently across reruns. The pattern weakens once the proxy-heavy inputs are removed, so it should be read cautiously.
